textPlain: Amazing work. I am the same age às the author and also would love to tinker with old hardware. This article taught me that I can do that with emulators all the while using modern developer tools! That’s very motivating, will see if I can get started. My first computer was a PC XT 386 IIRC, maybe I can do the same kind of thing on that.
I’ve never worked with assembly before, but I learned a lot from this article.
Or why robots are playing DOOM now. What programming for the Atari ST in 2025 has taught me.

We find inspiration where we can / want to.  I've been guiding a vanity vibe coding project and watching what it can do.  I used to love assembly and did inline optmizations for crypto operations at a company I was at years back.  These days it's more - well, that's interesting, I don't have time, what can the Claude/Apu/etc tell me.

Emulators are wonderful, I got into assembly for the 6502 processor used in the NES (Nintendo Entertainment System) and it's been an absolute blast, there is something so inherently satisfying and almost zen-like in it.

I never programmed assembly before 2025. For some reason i started tinkering with emulated 6502 cpu (same as in apple 2) and its FUN. 
In matters of weeks I managed to develop my own cpu architecture b, opcodes, wrote VM and assembler for it.Bare metal programming with those old CPUs is WAY easier than learning new web stack IMO.
